title: A Good Opportunity

jokes.text: Your cds made double platinum in America... No, it's the crazy dogggz, sorry.
jokes.setup: Murray announces amazing news about their CD sales going double platinum
jokes.punchline: It's immediately deflated - he was reading the wrong band's agenda
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/deflation structure with efficient delivery. Murray's bumbling manager character shines through.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Your cds made double platinum in America... No, it's the crazy dogggz, sorry.

jokes.text: I thought we had sold five.
jokes.setup: After hearing they went double platinum
jokes.punchline: Bret's expectation was they'd only sold five copies total
jokes.explanation: Economical line that perfectly captures the band's lack of success through Bret's modest expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I thought we had sold five.

jokes.text: They called me to say that you stole a cushion. - Did you? - Mm-hmm. - Yes.
jokes.setup: Murray asks if they stole a library cushion
jokes.punchline: They casually admit to the petty theft without shame
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor in the casual admission to such a small-scale theft, delivered with characteristic deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They called me to say that you stole a cushion.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Did you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: No, this is a really nice cushion.
jokes.setup: Murray tells them they can't steal cushions
jokes.punchline: Jemaine justifies the theft based on the cushion's quality
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective absurdist logic - quality justifies theft in Jemaine's mind.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, this is a really nice cushion.

jokes.text: R Kelly wants to sing on your next song... Yes, that's the crazy dogggz.
jokes.setup: Another piece of exciting news for the band
jokes.punchline: Again, it's meant for the other band Murray manages
jokes.explanation: Callback to the earlier mix-up, establishing the running gag of Murray's confusion between bands.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: R Kelly wants to sing on your next song... Yes, that's the crazy dogggz.

jokes.text: 'dear Murray, We want to fire you as our manager.' - What? - What?
jokes.setup: Murray reads what he thinks is another mix-up from his agenda
jokes.punchline: This time it's actually meant for him - the band is firing him
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of the established pattern - this time the agenda item is actually for him.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: dear Murray, We want to fire you as our manager.
What?
What?

jokes.text: Now look at you. - We're poor and we've got no gigs. - We're slightly poorer.
jokes.setup: Murray claims he's improved their situation since they hired him
jokes.punchline: They're in exactly the same position, but slightly worse
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery that gets funnier with the escalation to 'slightly poorer.' Great character work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Now look at you.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We're poor and we've got no gigs.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We're slightly poorer.

jokes.text: Yeah, bret's only got one shoe.
jokes.setup: Evidence of their worsening financial situation
jokes.punchline: The specific visual detail of Bret literally missing one shoe
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual gag that makes their poverty concrete and absurd. The single shoe is perfectly pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, bret's only got one shoe.

jokes.text: I can get you another shoe... it's not about the shoe... Not a problem. What size are you?
jokes.setup: Bret insists the firing isn't about his missing shoe
jokes.punchline: Murray completely ignores this and fixates on solving the shoe problem
jokes.explanation: Murray's character perfectly displayed - missing the big picture while fixating on a minor detail he can 'fix.'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I can get you another shoe...

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: it's not about the shoe...

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Not a problem. What size are you?

jokes.text: Item four: Stuff you.
jokes.setup: Murray pretends to find another agenda item after being fired
jokes.punchline: His professional meeting format continues even while telling them off
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - Murray maintains his formal meeting structure even while having a breakdown.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Item four: Stuff you.

jokes.text: Are you sure that's not for the crazy dogggz?
jokes.setup: Murray delivers his angry 'stuff you' message
jokes.punchline: Jemaine deadpans asking if this anger is also meant for the other band
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that subverts the moment's anger with characteristic deadpan logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you sure that's not for the crazy dogggz?

jokes.text: Why did I get double stuffed?
jokes.setup: Murray says 'stuff you' to both, then 'stuff you again, jemaine'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine calmly questions the unfair distribution of insults
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's deadpan concern about getting more insults is perfectly in character and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why did I get double stuffed?

jokes.text: They're fake. I had to make them myself... They're pencil sharpeners stuck To a couple of bits of wood
jokes.setup: Murray reveals their gold records are fake
jokes.punchline: The pathetic DIY nature of the fake awards - pencil sharpeners on wood
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual detail that makes Murray's deception both pathetic and endearing. The specificity sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They're fake. I had to make them myself... They're pencil sharpeners stuck To a couple of bits of wood

jokes.text: I thought we won 'best new zealand artists.' There's no such category, jemaine.
jokes.setup: The revelation that their Grammy was fake
jokes.punchline: Jemaine believed in the ultra-specific, obviously made-up category
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Jemaine's naivety about the obviously fake award category.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I thought we won 'best new zealand artists.'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: There's no such category, jemaine.

jokes.text: 'Doggy bounce,' number one. 'Doggy dance,' number five. 'In the pound,' number 37.
jokes.setup: Murray bragging about Crazy Dogggz's success
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous song titles and the declining chart positions
jokes.explanation: The absurd dog-themed song titles and descending chart positions show the 'hit machine' is actually declining.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'Doggy bounce,' number one. 'Doggy dance,' number five. 'In the pound,' number 37.

jokes.text: What about your 'best management' award? That's fake as well.
jokes.setup: Revealing all their awards are fake
jokes.punchline: Murray's own fake award for his terrible management
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic topper where Murray has also faked an award for himself, despite being demonstrably terrible at management.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What about your 'best management' award? That's fake as well.

jokes.text: Good luck without me, you turkeys.
jokes.setup: Murray's dramatic exit after being fired
jokes.punchline: His choice of 'turkeys' as an insult undermines the dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Murray's mild insult choice perfectly captures his inability to be truly mean, even when angry.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Good luck without me, you turkeys.

jokes.text: ♪ rejected ♪ ♪ thrown away ♪ ♪ affected ♪ ♪ I don't know what to say ♪
jokes.setup: Murray begins an emotional song about being fired
jokes.punchline: The forced rhyme scheme and melodramatic lyrics
jokes.explanation: Murray's attempt at an emotional song is endearingly amateur, establishing the musical comedy format.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ rejected ♪ ♪ thrown away ♪ ♪ affected ♪ ♪ I don't know what to say ♪

jokes.text: ♪ like a cake shop without any cakes ♪ ♪ like a cornflake box without any flakes ♪
jokes.setup: Murray continues his metaphor-heavy sad song
jokes.punchline: The increasingly specific and silly similes
jokes.explanation: The absurd specificity of the metaphors (cornflake box without flakes) is both silly and endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ like a cake shop without any cakes ♪ ♪ like a cornflake box without any flakes ♪

jokes.text: ♪ like a little orphan girl... ♪ Murray? One moment. ♪ and rejected. ♪
jokes.setup: Murray is in the middle of his emotional song
jokes.punchline: He's interrupted by work, professionally responds, then continues the song
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing - the interruption and Murray's professional response mid-breakdown is brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ like a little orphan girl... ♪
Murray?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: One moment.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ and rejected. ♪

jokes.text: Murray? One moment. ¶ and rejected. ¶ Yes, Glen?
jokes.setup: Murray in the middle of his dramatic song
jokes.punchline: Interruption for a business call, then finishing the song
jokes.explanation: Perfect interruption comedy - Murray pauses his dramatic song for a mundane business moment then resumes.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: One moment.
and rejected.

dialogue.speaker: Glen
dialogue.line: Yes, Murray?

jokes.text: Please, tell him I'm not there. I'm not here either. I'm not... not anywhere.
jokes.setup: Murray tries to avoid a call from Poland
jokes.punchline: His logic escalates to claiming he doesn't exist anywhere
jokes.explanation: Murray's escalating logic about his non-existence is endearingly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Please, tell him I'm not there. I'm not here either. I'm not... not anywhere.

jokes.text: They'll never hit the big time. They're too devious. Trust me, though, they're devious. I know.
jokes.setup: Murray watches another band perform to a good crowd
jokes.punchline: His bizarrely specific criticism that they're 'devious' with no explanation
jokes.explanation: Murray's insistence on 'devious' as a career-limiting trait is perfectly absurd and in character.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They'll never hit the big time. They're too devious. Trust me, though, they're devious. I know.

jokes.text: What would you be doing behind the scenes? - Having dinner? - Going to the movies? - Relaxing?
jokes.setup: Murray claims he did important work 'behind the scenes'
jokes.punchline: They suggest increasingly mundane personal activities
jokes.explanation: Their deadpan suggestions deflate Murray's claims of important work with obvious personal activities.

dialogue.line: What would you be doing behind the scenes?
Having dinner?
Going to the movies?
Relaxing?

jokes.text: Um, higgling. - Higgling? - Yeah, do you know what that means? - Is that a word that you just made up? - It's made-up, yeah
jokes.setup: Murray tries to impress them with business terms like 'haggling' and 'higgling'
jokes.punchline: He immediately admits 'higgling' is a word he just invented
jokes.explanation: Murray's casual admission that he made up a professional-sounding word is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Um, higgling.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Higgling?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, do you know what that means?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Is that a word that you just made up?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's made-up, yeah

jokes.text: This could make you so rich you'll be shitting money. Literally if you wanted.
jokes.setup: Martin promises they'll be very rich from the jingle
jokes.punchline: The bizarre escalation to literal money defecation as an option
jokes.explanation: Absurd advertising speak taken to a literal extreme. The 'if you wanted' makes it even more bizarre.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: This could make you so rich you'll be shitting money. Literally if you wanted.

jokes.text: But we can't sell it tonight 'cause I'm wearing it.
jokes.setup: Murray shows off their band merchandise t-shirt
jokes.punchline: The immediate practical problem with their merchandising strategy
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of an obvious business problem they hadn't considered.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But we can't sell it tonight 'cause I'm wearing it.

jokes.text: How'd you get the jacket so realistic? - Yeah, that's real denim.
jokes.setup: Bret admires the realistic jacket on Murray's doll
jokes.punchline: Murray's matter-of-fact explanation that he used actual denim on a tiny doll
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of using actual denim for a tiny doll, delivered completely seriously.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How'd you get the jacket so realistic?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's real denim.

jokes.text: here's two men Who know exactly what women feel like.
jokes.setup: Martin explains why they're perfect for the women's toothpaste jingle
jokes.punchline: The obvious irony of two clueless men understanding women
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic humor - the ad executive's completely wrong assessment of their qualifications.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: here's two men Who know exactly what women feel like.

jokes.text: Underscore is your middle name, is it?
jokes.setup: Martin gives his email as martinóclark@aol.com
jokes.punchline: Bret interprets the underscore literally as part of his name
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan misunderstanding of technology. Bret's literal interpretation is brilliantly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Underscore is your middle name, is it?

jokes.text: ♪ some women like men ♪ ♪ some are lesbian ♪ ♪ femident toothpaste. ♪
jokes.setup: They try to write a jingle about women
jokes.punchline: Their reductive and obvious observations set to music
jokes.explanation: Their attempt at understanding women results in the most basic possible observations.

dialogue.line: ♪ some women like men ♪
♪ some are lesbian ♪
♪ femident toothpaste. ♪

jokes.text: Nah. Weaving is a man's game... I come from a family of weavers.
jokes.setup: Jemaine suggests women love weaving
jokes.punchline: Bret's bizarre insistence that weaving is masculine, citing his family history
jokes.explanation: Bret's passionate defense of weaving as masculine is absurdly specific and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Nah. Weaving is a man's game... I come from a family of weavers.

jokes.text: I thought your dad was a sheep lawyer. Yeah, during the day, but at night he weaves a lot.
jokes.setup: Jemaine remembers Bret's dad's unusual profession
jokes.punchline: The idea of a sheep lawyer who weaves as a hobby at night
jokes.explanation: The combination of 'sheep lawyer' and nighttime weaving creates a perfectly absurd character background.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I thought your dad was a sheep lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, during the day, but at night he weaves a lot.

jokes.text: Women's rights. - No, that's more of a man's thing, isn't it? ... my father's a women's rights activist. Dad wouldn't allow that.
jokes.setup: They discuss whether women's rights appeal to women
jokes.punchline: Bret's father is a women's rights activist who won't let his wife participate
jokes.explanation: Brilliant ironic contradiction - a women's rights activist who controls his wife. Perfect absurdist logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Women's rights.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, that's more of a man's thing, isn't it? ... my father's a women's rights activist. Dad wouldn't allow that.

jokes.text: They like wrestling in cooking oil on a plane... I took them up on a plane, they were making a salad, Next thing you know, they're just wrestling at 85,000 feet.
jokes.setup: Dave offers his expertise about what women like
jokes.punchline: His bizarrely specific and unlikely anecdote about airborne oil wrestling
jokes.explanation: Dave's completely made-up story is absurd but delivered with such confidence it becomes funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: They like wrestling in cooking oil on a plane... I took them up on a plane, they were making a salad, Next thing you know, they're just wrestling at 85,000 feet.

jokes.text: They're probably just doing brazilians. They'll show up. Don't worry.
jokes.setup: Dave explains why there are no women at his party
jokes.punchline: His casual assumption about their grooming schedules
jokes.explanation: Dave's clueless confidence about women's behavior continues his character arc.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: They're probably just doing brazilians. They'll show up. Don't worry.

jokes.text: It's easier than easy. - Oh, okay, so you're telling me It was unbelievably easy.
jokes.setup: Murray tries to prove they need him, they say managing themselves is easy
jokes.punchline: Murray's logical escalation that it was 'unbelievably easy'
jokes.explanation: Murray's frustrated logic as he tries to process how little they need him is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's easier than easy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, okay, so you're telling me It was unbelievably easy.

jokes.text: Monday you're trying to do a TV commercial, They're filming it, it's all about toothpaste, Then Tuesday they're filming you naked.
jokes.setup: Murray warns about the dangers of the advertising world
jokes.punchline: His specific timeline of how quickly things escalate to nudity
jokes.explanation: Murray's paranoid but specific timeline of advertising corruption is endearingly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Monday you're trying to do a TV commercial, They're filming it, it's all about toothpaste, Then Tuesday they're filming you naked.

jokes.text: This cover version came out 13 years Before the crazy dogggz's original version.
jokes.setup: Murray explains the plagiarism problem with Crazy Dogggz
jokes.punchline: The obvious logical impossibility delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of an impossible situation. Murray's matter-of-fact tone makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: This cover version came out 13 years Before the crazy dogggz's original version.

jokes.text: So I've got... so you're normal... - No, I'm bad. - You're bad? - I've got three...
jokes.setup: Murray tries to tally their opinions on whether the situation is bad
jokes.punchline: Murray gets confused by his own simple polling system
jokes.explanation: Murray's inability to handle basic counting/categorizing is perfect character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: So I've got... so you're normal...

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I'm bad.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're bad?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've got three...

jokes.text: That's worth $20,000. It was a gift from Tori Amos.
jokes.setup: Murray protects something during his office move
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous claim about an expensive gift from Tori Amos
jokes.explanation: Murray's specific and unlikely celebrity connection claim is perfectly in character for his delusions.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's worth $20,000. It was a gift from Tori Amos.

jokes.text: You should've seen Doug When he heard what happened. - Hey. - He went berserk. - Really? Actually, mel really went more sort of berserk.
jokes.setup: Doug describing his reaction to the news
jokes.punchline: Mel corrects him that she went more berserk, competing over who was more upset
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Mel and Doug's competitive dynamic even in supporting Murray.

dialogue.line: You should've seen Doug When he heard what happened.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Hey.
He went berserk.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Really?
Actually, mel really went more sort of berserk.

jokes.text: Conversation hog. 'Oink oink oink oink. I've got something to say all the time.' He's always talking.
jokes.setup: Mel accusing Doug of hogging conversation
jokes.punchline: Mel's elaborate pig impression and continued talking while accusing Doug of talking too much
jokes.explanation: Mel's ironic accusation about Doug talking too much while she dominates the conversation with pig noises.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Conversation hog. 'Oink oink oink oink. I've got something to say all the time.' He's always talking.

jokes.text: Why did you come, Doug, If you didn't want to light a fire?
jokes.setup: Doug expresses concern about the legality of street fires
jokes.punchline: Mel's frustrated logic about Doug's commitment to the plan
jokes.explanation: Mel's aggressive frustration with Doug's reasonable concerns establishes their relationship dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Why did you come, Doug, If you didn't want to light a fire?

jokes.text: Some of the best songs are jingles. ♪ pussymart, pussymart. ♪
jokes.setup: Dave tries to convince them that jingles are legitimate music
jokes.punchline: His example of a great jingle is for a store with an inappropriate name
jokes.explanation: Dave's confident delivery of a clearly inappropriate jingle as an example of quality.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Some of the best songs are jingles. ♪ pussymart, pussymart. ♪

jokes.text: The d in my name stands for 'deal.' ... the word 'Dave' Actually means 'deal' in latino?
jokes.setup: Dave explains his credentials as a negotiator
jokes.punchline: His made-up etymology about his name and 'latino' as a language
jokes.explanation: Dave's confident but completely wrong linguistic claims establish his character as a BS artist.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: The d in my name stands for 'deal.' ... the word 'Dave' Actually means 'deal' in latino?

jokes.text: How much would you give me for this? - A dollar. - Double it... - Sold! I just made him pay Nearly 10 times what he wanted to pay
jokes.setup: Dave demonstrates his 'double-down technique'
jokes.punchline: His math is completely wrong but he's proud of the 'manipulation'
jokes.explanation: Dave's proud incompetence at basic math while claiming business genius is perfect character comedy.

dialogue.line: How much would you give me for this?

dialogue.speaker: Bret

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I just made him pay Nearly 10 times what he wanted to pay

jokes.text: I thought it was an exercise. - Yeah, you've got to learn the whole lesson.

jokes.punchline: Dave insists the real money exchange is part of the lesson
jokes.explanation: Dave's commitment to his scam even when called out shows his shameless character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I thought it was an exercise.

jokes.text: This song is 18 minutes long. We're talking about a 30-second commercial here.
jokes.setup: The ad executives review their jingle submission
jokes.punchline: The absurd length mismatch between their song and commercial needs

jokes.punchline: Martin's dismissive Lord of the Rings reference based on their nationality

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We thought it would be more of a concept.

dialogue.line: I know you're from new zealand. What is this, 'lord of the rings'?

jokes.text: some of them aren't about toothpaste, They're just things in our life.
jokes.setup: The executives point out the lyrics don't fit the product
jokes.punchline: Bret's honest admission that they just sang about random personal stuff
jokes.explanation: Bret's guileless honesty about their unprofessional approach is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: some of them aren't about toothpaste, They're just things in our life.

jokes.text: The fee is $1,000 each. - Sorry, now I'll say it. Double it. - I am doubling it. It was going to be $500 each.

jokes.punchline: Jemaine's deadpan observation about the mental doubling
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection about the doubling and Jemaine's matter-of-fact observation about Martin's mental process.

jokes.punchline: It's just his car's glovebox, implying he's living in his car
jokes.explanation: Subtle visual gag revealing Murray's desperate circumstances through euphemistic language.

jokes.punchline: His mispronunciation creates yacht racing terminology that Jemaine interprets literally
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay combining Murray's pretentious Latin and Jemaine's literal interpretation. Perfect economy and character work.

jokes.text: Unless you move your vehicle every three hours.
jokes.setup: Murray denies living in his car because it's illegal
jokes.punchline: He knows the specific legal loophole that would allow it
jokes.explanation: Murray's knowledge of this specific legal detail reveals he's clearly researched living in his car.

dialogue.line: Unless you move your vehicle every three hours.

jokes.setup: Murray complimenting Bret's pants
jokes.punchline: Bret's literal interpretation of 'man-made' vs handwoven
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the weaving discussion with Bret's deadpan misinterpretation of manufacturing terminology.

dialogue.line: ♪ you are a woman, you wear women's wear ♪

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: ♪ you have breasts and longish hair, oh yeah ♪

jokes.text: Is it just a bit of card? ... Just a card, a greenish card? ... Is it like a library card?
jokes.setup: They're told they need green cards to work
jokes.punchline: Bret's literal interpretation of what a 'green card' might be
jokes.explanation: Bret's innocent confusion about immigration documents, thinking it's literally a green-colored card.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is it just a bit of card? ... Just a card, a greenish card? ... Is it like a library card?

jokes.text: No, we're illegal immigrants.
jokes.setup: Asked if they're legally allowed to work in the country
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's casual admission to being illegal immigrants
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's matter-of-fact admission to illegal status delivered with characteristic nonchalance.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, we're illegal immigrants.

jokes.text: We've got blue robes on. We're not wearing anything...
jokes.setup: Murray asks what they're wearing while they're in trouble
jokes.punchline: They're apparently in bathrobes with nothing underneath during a crisis
jokes.explanation: The image of them dealing with legal trouble while barely clothed adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We've got blue robes on. We're not wearing anything...

jokes.text: Bad news, guys: Your passports aren't ready. - Do you have them? - No, they're in my cupboard. - You just said you had them. - I've got them in my cupboard.
jokes.setup: Murray claims to have solved their passport problem
jokes.punchline: His circular logic about having them but not having them ready
jokes.explanation: Murray's confused explanation reveals his inability to actually help despite his claims.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Bad news, guys: Your passports aren't ready.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you have them?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, they're in my cupboard.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You just said you had them.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've got them in my cupboard.

jokes.text: This is exactly what happens when you try to manage yourselves. If you had stuck with me, you wouldn't be in this job situation, would you?
jokes.setup: They're in trouble and need his help
jokes.punchline: Murray uses their crisis to argue for his management skills despite causing the problem
jokes.explanation: Murray's ability to spin his own failure as evidence they need him is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: This is exactly what happens when you try to manage yourselves. If you had stuck with me, you wouldn't be in this job situation, would you?

jokes.text: I think we should run for it. Come on, let's go. Go! Run! Run! Quickly! No time for debate!
jokes.setup: Murray's professional management advice for their legal situation
jokes.punchline: His solution is to literally run away from immigration officials
jokes.explanation: Murray's 'professional' management advice is to flee from authorities, showing his approach to problem-solving.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I think we should run for it. Come on, let's go. Go! Run! Run! Quickly! No time for debate!

jokes.text: I managed to get you a shoe, eh?
jokes.setup: After their chaotic escape from the commercial shoot
jokes.punchline: Murray proudly delivers on his earlier promise to get Bret a shoe
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that shows Murray's priorities - he solved the shoe problem while everything else fell apart.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I managed to get you a shoe, eh?

jokes.text: Good managing, eh, guys? - Murray, it couldn't have possibly gone worse.
jokes.setup: Murray proudly asking for validation of his management
jokes.punchline: Bret's matter-of-fact assessment that it was the worst possible outcome
jokes.explanation: Perfect contradiction between Murray's pride and the objective reality of complete disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Good managing, eh, guys?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Murray, it couldn't have possibly gone worse.

jokes.text: I think there's a guardian angel looking after us. - Somewhere up there, someone's looking down, Making sure everything turns out okay for us.
jokes.setup: Murray's optimistic belief about divine protection after total disaster
jokes.punchline: Sets up the angels having sex song
jokes.explanation: Murray's misguided optimism sets up the subversive angels song perfectly, great ironic setup.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I think there's a guardian angel looking after us. - Somewhere up there, someone's looking down, Making sure everything turns out okay for us.

jokes.text: ♪ there are angels ♪ ♪ in the clouds ♪ ♪ doing it ♪
jokes.setup: Murray mentions a guardian angel looking after them
jokes.punchline: Their song imagines angels having sex in the clouds
jokes.explanation: Their innocent interpretation of Murray's guardian angel comment becomes a sexually charged song concept.

dialogue.line: ♪ there are angels ♪ ♪ in the clouds ♪ ♪ doing it ♪

jokes.text: Angels having sex in the clouds song
jokes.setup: Murray's innocent comment about guardian angels
jokes.punchline: Song reveals the angels are having sex, making rain from their activities
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly subversive musical number that takes Murray's innocent guardian angel comment and turns it into absurd celestial sexuality.

dialogue.line: Angels having sex in the clouds song

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine fire Murray for neglecting them in favour of the Crazy Dogggz and almost immediately they receive an offer to write a jingle for a commercial. Meanwhile Murray's fortunes reverse after he discovers that the Crazy Dogggz big hit is a rip-off of a 1990s Polish song.
